results.possibleIdentity: Michael Wisniewski was an engineer and technical collaborator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a member of the technical staff at Bell Telephone Laboratories, the institution where many of E.A.T.'s core engineering members originated. Wisniewski is specifically credited as a member of the technical team for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a major E.A.T. project that integrated art and advanced technology. His work involved the complex engineering and technical systems required for the pavilion's immersive environment.

Ray Wisniewski (Raymond Wisniewski) was a prominent underground filmmaker and experimental artist active in New York City during the 1960s and 1970s. He was a significant figure in the avant-garde scene, with his films such as 'Doomshow' (1966) being programmed by Jonas Mekas at the Film-Makers' Cinematheque and listed in seminal works like Sheldon Renan's 'An Introduction to the American Underground Film' (1967). His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) includes the inclusion of his experimental works in historical retrospectives and multimedia programs organized or curated by the organization to highlight the development of avant-garde film and technology.

Marek Wiśniewski is a Polish conceptual and experimental artist who rose to prominence in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a participant in the influential 1970 Wroclaw exhibition of conceptual art and was active in the international mail art and neo-avant-garde movements. His work often explored themes of communication and system-based art, paralleling the goals of organizations like E.A.T. and the 'Information' era of conceptualism.

results.possibleIdentity: Harry Harper was an artist and programmer associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his contribution to the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World's Fair (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan, which was a major collaborative project organized by E.A.T. Harper was one of the thirty American and Japanese 'programmers' (artists and researchers) invited to create live events for the Pavilion. His specific contribution was an immersive sound environment created within the Pavilion's Mirror Dome, which utilized some of the first recordings of humpback whale songs. These recordings were made by his colleague, the noted bioacoustician Roger Payne.

William Harper (born 1944) is an American studio jeweler, enamelist, and painter who became active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally trained as an abstract painter at the Cleveland Institute of Art, he transitioned into enameling and studio craft jewelry in the mid-1960s. His work is noted for its complexity, often incorporating non-precious materials like bone, teeth, and plastic alongside precious metals and gemstones. While his primary fame lies in the field of studio jewelry and enameling, he was active during the era of E.A.T. and his work is held in major museum collections, including the Metropolitan Museum of Art and the Victoria and Albert Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: Elaine Summers (1925–2014) was a pioneering American choreographer, filmmaker, and intermedia artist. She was a founding member of the Judson Dance Theater in New York City during the early 1960s, a group that helped redefine modern dance. Summers is recognized as a pioneer in the field of intermedia, which explores the intersection of different artistic disciplines and technology. Her seminal work, "Fantastic Gardens" (1964), was one of the first multi-media performances to integrate dance, film projections, and music. In 1968, she founded the Experimental Intermedia Foundation (EIF), which remains a notable organization for promoting avant-garde artistic collaborations. Her work and foundation are frequently associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, and she is often cited alongside E.A.T. figures such as Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg in historical accounts of early new media and technology-based performance art.

Carol Summers (1925–2016) was a distinguished American printmaker best known for his large-scale, color-saturated woodcuts and innovative relief printing techniques. While primarily known for his visual art, he was an active participant in the avant-garde New York art scene of the 1960s. He is specifically linked to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his participation in the landmark 1966 event "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering." Summers was a performer in Yvonne Rainer's choreographed piece "Carriage Discreteness," which utilized remote-controlled objects and electronic communication systems. His involvement in this foundational E.A.T. project places him within the circle of artists who pioneered collaborations between the arts and engineering during that era.

Gene Summers (1928–2011) was an American architect and artist who began his career as a protégé and supervising architect for Ludwig Mies van der Rohe. He was the architect for several major projects, including the McCormick Place Convention Center in Chicago, completed in 1970. Later in his career, he turned increasingly toward sculpture and fine art, working in various media including bronze. While his professional career as an architect was his primary focus during the late 1960s and early 1970s, he is often identified as an artist in architectural and design contexts and was active during the period of high interest in the intersection of structural engineering and aesthetic form.

results.possibleIdentity: Elaine Summers (1925–2014) was a pioneering American choreographer, experimental filmmaker, and intermedia artist who was a key figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1960s and 1970s. A founding member of the Judson Dance Theater in 1962, she is widely credited with being one of the first artists to fuse dance with experimental film and technology, a practice she termed 'filmdance.' In 1964, she created 'Fantastic Gardens,' which is considered a landmark work in the evolution of intermedia art for its use of multiple film projections and environmental immersion. In 1968, she founded the Experimental Intermedia Foundation (EIF), an organization that mirrored the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) by fostering cross-disciplinary collaborations between artists, musicians, and technologists. Summers' work often explored the interrelationship between art and technology; she was a Fellow at MIT's Center for Advanced Visual Studies and collaborated with notable figures like Stan VanDerBeek and Nam June Paik. She is also the creator of Kinetic Awareness, a somatic bodywork method involving the use of rubber balls to increase physical self-awareness and release tension. Her notable works from the late 1960s and 1970s include 'Walking Dance for Any #' (1968), 'Energy Changes' (1973), and 'Illuminated Workingman' (1976). Her professional archives are now held at the New York Public Library's Jerome Robbins Dance Division.

results.possibleIdentity: Morris Kronfeld (1914–2011) was a New York Modernist artist and educator active throughout the mid-to-late 20th century, including the 1960s and 1970s. He studied biology and art history at Brooklyn College and received a master's degree from NYU. Professionally, he served as an art teacher and eventually a district art supervisor for the New York City Board of Education. His artistic practice was eclectic, involving experiments with materials such as sand mixed with paint to create unique textures, pen and ink (often using pens he whittled from feathers and sticks), watercolors, and oils. His subject matter ranged from surreal cityscapes and architectural fantasies to botanical studies and abstract forms. Given his New York location, his career timeline, and his interdisciplinary background in biology and art, he is a strong candidate for an artist who would have been associated with or interested in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement in New York during the late 1960s.

The Kronfeld Aviation Art Society was an organization of artists founded in London in 1966, named after the pioneer glider pilot Robert Kronfeld. The group was composed of artists (approximately 10 at its founding) who specialized in the depiction of aircraft and aeronautical themes, a field that inherently required a close relationship between art and engineering. The society was active until 1971, when it was dissolved and reorganized into the Guild of Aviation Artists. Its first major exhibition was held at the Royal Aeronautical Society in 1971. The society's focus on technical and scientific subjects (aviation and aeronautics) aligns with the period of activity and the technical collaborative spirit of organizations like E.A.T.

Peter C. Kronfeld was a prominent ophthalmologist and professor whose work and presence intersected with the avant-garde design and art communities of the 1960s. He was a featured speaker at the 1961 International Design Conference in Aspen (IDCA), a precursor and parallel venue to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) ethos, where participants explored the 'cross-fertilization of ideas' between art, design, and science. His 1943 book, 'The Human Eye in Anatomical Transparencies,' utilized a 'trans-vision' process of layered transparent acetate to synthesize anatomical data into a three-dimensional representation, which was recognized by design historians as a significant innovation in technical and artistic visualization. Peter Kronfeld is listed in 'The Aspen Papers' (1974) among other contributors to these interdisciplinary dialogues, which included E.A.T. founders like John Cage.

results.possibleIdentity: Paul H. Deal (also known as Paul Deal) was a scientist, specifically a microbiologist and plant physiologist, active at the NASA Ames Research Center during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is known for his work in exobiology and biological simulations. Deal co-authored several significant scientific papers during this period, including 'Altered gravitational field effects on the fertilized frog egg' (1970) and research on bacteria under simulated Martian conditions (1964). Given the objective of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) to facilitate collaborations between artists and scientists, it is highly probable that he served as a scientific or engineering collaborator on projects during that era. In later years, he developed the computer simulation program and text-based RPG 'Fallthru' (1988), which he originally created as a learning project to build biological simulations.

Peter Deal was a British photographer active in the late 1960s, notably working in both New York and London. In 1969, he was described as a fashion and beauty perfectionist and a master black-and-white printer who had trained under prominent photographers like Irving Penn and Bert Stern. He is historically associated with high-profile photography projects, including contributing to 'The Last Sitting' with Marilyn Monroe. Given his presence in the New York art and photography scene during the late 1960s, he is a candidate for the identity of 'P. Deal' as a visual artist.
